perturbation.original_text: Guarantors shall pay or cause to be paid to Franchisor all monies payable by Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement on the days and times and in the manner therein appointed for payment thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Guarantors shall endeavor to pay or cause to be paid to Franchisor all monies payable by Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall pay' to 'shall endeavor to pay' weakens the guarantee, potentially violating laws requiring guarantors to fulfill obligations. This introduces ambiguity and makes the guarantor's responsibility less certain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds - Requirement of Unconditional Guarantee
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements, including guarantees, to be in writing and to clearly express the obligation. Changing 'shall pay' to 'shall endeavor to pay' creates ambiguity and might be interpreted as not establishing a clear, unconditional guarantee, thereby potentially rendering the guarantee unenforceable under New York law if the franchisor's principal place of business is in NY.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall pay' to 'shall endeavor to pay' weakens the obligation of the guarantor. New York General Obligations Law Section 5-701 requires agreements to answer for the debt of another to be in writing. The original text clearly states the guarantor's obligation to pay. The changed text introduces ambiguity, making the guarantor's responsibility less certain, potentially rendering the guarantee unenforceable under New York law.

perturbation.original_text: Guarantors shall unconditionally guarantee full performance and discharge by Master Franchisee of all the obligations of Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement at the times and in the manner therein provided.
perturbation.changed_text: Guarantors shall guarantee performance and discharge by Master Franchisee of the obligations of Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'unconditionally' from the guarantee clause weakens the surety, which may violate state laws demanding guarantees to be unequivocal and absolute.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law (Civil Code § 2806) implies that a surety's obligation is unconditional unless explicitly stated otherwise. Removing the word 'unconditionally' introduces potential ambiguity about the surety's commitment, creating a legal vulnerability in California if the franchisor has its principal place of business there. A judge could interpret the altered clause to suggest conditions on the guarantee, potentially excusing the guarantor from their obligation.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 13. SURETYSHIP [2787 - 2856] ( Heading of Title 13 amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453. ) ARTICLE 4. Liability of Sureties [2806 - 2811] ( Heading of Article 4 amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453. ) 2806. A suretyship obligation is to be deemed unconditional unless its terms import some condition precedent to the liability of the surety. (Amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453.)
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'unconditionally' weakens the guarantee, potentially contradicting California Civil Code § 2806, which states that a suretyship obligation is deemed unconditional unless its terms specify a condition precedent. The changed text introduces ambiguity about the surety's commitment, as it no longer explicitly states the guarantee is unconditional.

perturbation.original_text: H. This Guarantee shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of the state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated. In the event of any conflict of law, the laws of such state shall prevail, without regard to the application of such state's conflict of law rules. If, however, any provision of this Guarantee would not be enforceable under the laws of the state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated, and if the Master Franchisee's franchised business is located outside of such state and such provision would be enforceable under the laws of the state in which the Master Franchisee's franchised business is located, then such provision shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of that state. Any action brought to enforce or interpret this Guarantee in any court, whether federal or state, shall be brought within the county and state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated, and Guarantors hereby waive all questions of personal jurisdiction or venue for the purpose of carrying out this provision.
perturbation.changed_text: H. This Guarantee shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of the state in which the Master Franchisee's franchised business is located. Any action brought to enforce or interpret this Guarantee in any court, whether federal or state, shall be brought within the county and state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated, and Guarantors hereby waive all questions of personal jurisdiction or venue for the purpose of carrying out this provision.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the phrase 'This Guarantee shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of the state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated. In the event of any conflict of law, the laws of such state shall prevail, without regard to the application of such state's conflict of law rules. If, however, any provision of this Guarantee would not be enforceable under the laws of the state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated, and if the Master Franchisee's franchised business is located outside of such state and such provision would be enforceable under the laws of the state in which the Master Franchisee's franchised business is located, then such provision shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of that state.' This limits the governing law solely to the location of the franchisee's business. This would mean that certain protections afforded to franchisors, such as forum selection clauses, might be challenged or deemed unenforceable if the franchisor's state law provides stronger protections than the franchisee's state, in contravention of general contract law principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Freedom to Contract and Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 affirms the principle of party autonomy in choosing the governing law, but emphasizes that such a choice will not be given effect if it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. Removing the clause that allows the franchisor's state law to govern (with exceptions) and solely focusing on the franchisee's state could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent stronger franchisor protections, potentially violating the policy of states that heavily regulate franchise agreements to protect franchisors and undermining the franchisor's freedom to contract under their preferred legal framework.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause specifying the governing law as that of the franchisor's principal place of business, and the limitations on that clause, directly contradicts the principle of party autonomy in contract law, as explained by the LLM. This principle allows parties to choose the governing law of their contract, subject to limitations. The revised text eliminates this choice, potentially violating the policy of states that protect franchisors and undermining their freedom to contract under their preferred legal framework. Although the provided snippets do not directly state this, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation detail that omitting the original clause that gives the franchisor's state law preference, along with limitations, could circumvent franchisor protections. The new clause solely focuses on the franchisee's state.
